result.reasoning:
Verified both files at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

APPROPRIATE: The fix closes the build-time supply-chain window correctly and idiomatically.
- requirements-build.lock exists, was generated by `uv pip compile ... --generate-hashes`, and pins hatchling==1.30.1 plus its full transitive build deps (editables, packaging, pathspec, pluggy, trove-classifiers), each with two sha256 hashes.
- ci.yml installs it with `pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock` followed by `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` in all three install lanes (core test job L34-36, full test job L80-82, license/SBOM job L198-200). With --no-build-isolation the editable build uses the already-hash-pinned hatchling instead of pip fetching an un-hashed hatchling into an ephemeral isolated env. --no-deps prevents runtime deps from being re-resolved live.
- A sync/reproducibility guard (L141-146) regenerates the build lock from `printf 'hatchling\neditables\n'` and diffs it, failing CI with an actionable error if the lock drifts. The header is stripped before diffing, matching the existing two lockfile guards. No regression to other install lanes.

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: Ran test_ci_pins_build_backend_and_disables_live_build_isolation — passes (1 passed). Then simulated a revert (removed the --no-build-isolation flag and the require-hashes build-lock install line in ci.yml); the test FAILED with `AssertionError: build isolation still live (#J-003)`. Restored the file afterward. The test asserts the live-isolation install string, the lock reference, lock existence, hatchling==/--hash presence, and absence of the old un-hashed install — so it genuinely gates the fix rather than passing vacuously.

Appropriate: _experiment_id (tracking.py lines 64-80) wraps create_experiment in try/except; on the concurrent-create race it re-fetches the now-existing experiment via get_experiment_by_name and returns its id instead of letting RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS propagate (which would yield run_id=None and silently drop all study metrics/artifacts). It correctly re-raises when the re-fetch still returns None, so genuine create failures are not swallowed. Broad except is justified/commented (noqa BLE001), scope is tight, no regression to the F-023 param-logging cleanup. Complete/discriminating: test_experiment_id_survives_concurrent_create_race (test_observability.py lines 126-149) drives a RacingClient that returns None on the first check, raises RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS on create, then returns the winner's experiment on re-fetch, asserting the id is returned not raised. Verified empirically: passes with the fix; when I reverted to the naive return str(client.create_experiment(...)) the test FAILED with the RuntimeError propagating. File restored to committed state (git diff clean). Note: tests must run under the project venv (.venv/bin/python); system python3 is too old (dataclass slots).

Read both changed files at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and the test. APPROPRIATE: ci.yml now replaces all three `pip install --no-deps -e .` invocations (jobs: core lint, real-backend mypy, and license/supply-chain) with `pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock` (hashed build backend) followed by `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .`. This closes the un-hashed live hatchling fetch / build-time code-exec window. requirements-build.lock is new, uv-compiled with --generate-hashes, fully pinned (hatchling==1.30.1, editables==0.6 + transitive deps packaging/pathspec/pluggy/trove-classifiers, all with --hash=sha256). A sync-check step was added that regenerates the build lock via `uv pip compile` and diffs it, preventing staleness — idiomatic and consistent with the existing requirements-core.lock sync guard. No regression: --no-deps preserves the ADR-0006 backend-isolation guard. COMPLETE/DISCRIMINATES: test passes on HEAD; reverting the install line back to `pip install --no-deps -e .` and removing requirements-build.lock makes it FAIL with "build isolation still live (#J-003)". The test also asserts the old `pip install --no-deps -e .\n` line is absent, hatchling==/--hash present, and the lock file exists — all directly tied to the fix. Working tree restored cleanly after the experiment; test green again.

Read the changed file /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py (lines 64-80) and the test at tests/test_observability.py:126.

APPROPRIATE: _experiment_id wraps client.create_experiment in try/except. On the concurrent-create race (RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS), instead of propagating — which would bubble out of start_run leaving run_id=None and silently dropping all metrics + provenance for the study — it re-fetches via get_experiment_by_name and returns the winner's experiment_id. Crucially, if the re-fetch still returns None (a genuine non-race failure), it re-raises, so real errors are not swallowed. Happy paths (existing experiment, clean create) are unchanged; no regression. Style matches codebase (explicit noqa: BLE001 with justification).

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: Verified by execution with .venv/bin/python (3.14; system python3 too old for slots dataclass). Test passes as-is (1 passed). I reverted the except-branch to a bare `return str(client.create_experiment(...))` and re-ran: the test FAILED with RuntimeError RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS at tracking line. Restored the file (git diff clean). The test drives _experiment_id directly with a RacingClient (first get_experiment_by_name=None, create_experiment raises RESOURCE_ALREADY_EXISTS, second get_experiment_by_name returns _Exp(experiment_id='exp-7')) and asserts the result is 'exp-7' — exactly the re-fetch path. Confirmed it fails if reverted.

Read the changed file scripts/capture_transcripts.py and the test tests/test_scripts.py::test_scrub_redacts_operator_display_name at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

APPROPRIATE: The fix matches the description exactly. _display_name_patterns() (lines 76-105) sources candidate names from both ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES (comma-split, stripped) and `git config user.name` (subprocess, returncode-checked, wrapped in a broad except so git absence never breaks capture). It is length-guarded (`if len(n) < 4: continue`) to avoid over-redacting short common words, and builds whitespace-flexible regexes via `re.compile(r"\b" + r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in n.split()) + r"\b")` so "Ada   Lovelace" with multiple spaces is also matched, with \b word boundaries and re.escape preventing regex injection. The patterns are precomputed at module load (_DISPLAY_NAME_RES) and applied in _scrub (lines 124-127) after the username/email/secret rules, which is the correct order (so it catches names left in cleartext beside already-redacted emails). Idiomatic and no regression: full tests/test_scripts.py (11 tests incl. bearer-token, invalid-utf8, license-gate) all pass on a clean tree.

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: With the fix in place the named test passes. I reverted only the J-002 application loop in _scrub and re-ran — the test FAILED with "'Ada Lovelace' is contained here: Author: Ada Lovelace <[REDACTED]>..." confirming it specifically asserts the display name is gone (and separately that the email is still scrubbed and the flexible-whitespace form is caught). The test correctly relies on _load() doing a fresh exec_module after monkeypatch.setenv, so module-level pattern computation picks up ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES.

Note: a transient failure of test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 I observed was an artifact of my own backup/restore overlap during the revert experiment; on the verified-clean working tree (git status empty) all 11 tests pass.

Verified at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. The fix in scripts/capture_transcripts.py:152-155 changes binary classification from "fails strict UTF-8 decode" to "contains a NUL byte" (b"\x00" in raw) and decodes text with errors="replace" so _scrub() always runs. This correctly closes the CV-O-1 fail-open: a stray non-UTF-8 byte inside an otherwise-text JSONL no longer routes the whole file to the verbatim/raw branch and re-leaks secrets/PII. NUL is a sound binary signal (valid JSON/JSONL is NUL-free, so it is always scrubbed; genuine binary still copies verbatim). No regression: test_copy_scrubbed_copies_binary_verbatim still passes and the full file (11 tests) passes.

Discrimination: I reverted the change to the pre-fix strict-decode (try raw.decode("utf-8") except UnicodeDecodeError: data=raw) version. test_copy_scrubbed_scrubs_file_with_invalid_utf8 FAILED (secret "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuv" copied raw), then passed after restoring — it is a true end-to-end guard on the changed code path. NOTE: the other named test, test_scrub_fails_closed_on_invalid_utf8_bytes, does NOT discriminate this fix — it pre-decodes the bytes itself with errors="replace" and calls the UNCHANGED _scrub(), so it passes against both fixed and reverted code. It is a harmless/redundant redaction test, not a guard on the decode-gating logic. Minor concern, not a blocker, because the first named test fully and correctly discriminates the fix. Restored file to a clean HEAD state afterward (git checkout).

Read the changed file (scripts/capture_transcripts.py lines 76-128) and test (tests/test_scripts.py lines 149-156), and ran revert-discrimination.

APPROPRIATE: _display_name_patterns() derives names from ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES (comma-split/stripped) and `git config user.name` via subprocess with timeout=5 and a broad exception guard so capture never breaks — idiomatic, mirroring the existing _username_pattern(). Length-guarded (len<4 skip) to avoid over-redacting common words. Whitespace-flexible pattern r"\b"+r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in n.split())+r"\b" correctly matches multi-space "Ada   Lovelace"; re.escape on each token prevents regex injection; \b boundaries limit false positives. Applied in _scrub after username/email rules per the documented ordering. Module-level _DISPLAY_NAME_RES computed once at import; the test's per-call _load re-imports so monkeypatched env is honored. No regression — email/username/secret scrubbing paths untouched.

COMPLETE/DISCRIMINATING: test passes on fixed code. When I removed ONLY the J-002 application loop in _scrub and re-ran in an ISOLATED pytest process, the test FAILED with `AssertionError: 'Ada Lovelace' is contained here: Author: Ada Lovelace <[REDACTED]>...`. (A first revert run falsely passed due to a stale module/.pyc cache from running reverted+original in the same interpreter; the clean isolated re-run discriminates correctly.) Test covers plain redaction, whitespace-flexible form, and confirms email is still scrubbed.

Minor non-blocking note: test exercises only the ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES path (not git user.name) and does not assert the short-name over-redaction guard — coverage nicety, not a defect. File restored to HEAD; tree clean.

APPROPRIATE: The fix is correct, idiomatic, and regression-free. The error hierarchy in src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py confirms the premise: PermanentReasoningError (line 70) and RateLimitError (line 74) both subclass ReasoningError (line 66). Therefore pytest.raises(ReasoningError) is satisfied by ALL THREE classes and cannot detect a mis-classification toward a decisive subclass. The new _assert_transient helper (lines 143-149) asserts `not isinstance(excinfo.value, (RateLimitError, PermanentReasoningError))`, which precisely closes that gap. The three transient code paths it guards genuinely raise the base ReasoningError: empty reply -> line 502; generic non-(429/529/401/403) status e.g. 500 -> line 498; SDK raises mid-stream -> caught and re-raised at line 489. No production code changed; test-only edit; no regression. The helper is reused across all three tests (DRY) and well-documented.

COMPLETE (DISCRIMINATES): Empirically proven. With current code all three named tests pass (.venv/bin/python -m pytest -> 3 passed). I then reverted the three transient mappings to decisive subclasses (line 502 ReasoningError->RateLimitError, line 498 ->PermanentReasoningError, line 489 ->PermanentReasoningError) and re-ran: all three tests FAILED on the _assert_transient assertion (e.g. "expected a transient ReasoningError, got PermanentReasoningError"). The old assertion would have stayed green under that revert; the new one fails. seam.py restored, git diff clean. The tests fail-on-revert exactly as required.

Fix at .github/workflows/ci.yml:202 drops --with-system: `pip-licenses --format=json > licenses.json`. pip-licenses now defaults to scoping the scan to the installed distributions, and the step installs only requirements.lock + the hashed build backend + the editable package (--no-deps), so the license gate covers exactly the pinned dependency set rather than the runner's system tooling — correct, idiomatic, aligned with the J-006/F-013 rationale. No --with-system remains anywhere in the workflow; the gate still invokes scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json, so no regression. The test tests/test_supply_chain.py:330 (test_ci_license_gate_scopes_to_dependency_set) reads the CI YAML and asserts the --with-system invocation is absent. It passes on the current tree; I reinjected --with-system via sed and the test FAILED (AssertionError at line 334), then restored the file (git diff clean). The test genuinely discriminates against the real regression. Minor non-blocking note: the assertion matches a fixed substring including flag order, so a differently-ordered revert could slip past — but that is not the original bug shape.
